refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-003 Refined: The NGHE solution shall enable operators and tele-operators to achieve a measured energy efficiency of energy_per_ton ≤ E_target (customer-defined threshold) over the agreed evaluation period, through integrated monitoring, control optimization, and real-time feedback mechanisms.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR is outcome-focused but lacks explicit linkage to the mechanisms (monitoring, control, feedback) that enable efficiency achievement. Refinement clarifies that energy efficiency is not a passive property but an active capability requiring measurement, visibility, and adaptive control. This supports both Site Manager accountability and Operator/Tele-operator decision-making.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure energy_per_ton = (total_energy_consumed_kWh / total_tonnage_moved) over a defined evaluation period (e.g., 1 week, 1 month). Compare against E_target. Acceptance: measured value ≤ E_target with ≥95% confidence over repeated evaluation cycles.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall continuously measure and log energy consumption (kWh) and work output (tonnage moved) at the vehicle level.
CR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall compute and display real-time energy_per_ton KPI to operators and tele-operators.
CR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall provide control recommendations or automated adjustments to minimize energy_per_ton without compromising safety or task completion.
CR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall log and report energy efficiency metrics to Site Manager for compliance verification and continuous improvement.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a centralized energy management system (EMS) that aggregates telemetry from vehicle sensors, computes energy_per_ton in real-time, and broadcasts KPI and control recommendations via a unified dashboard and teleoperation interface.
DP-B: Distribute energy monitoring and efficiency logic across vehicle onboard systems and tele-operator workstations, with periodic synchronization of metrics via network link.

DP-C: Implement a hybrid approach: vehicle-level energy metering and local optimization (DP logic on Vehicle), with centralized aggregation, KPI computation, and operator feedback (DP logic on Teleoperation and Safety Services).
DP-D: Implement energy efficiency as a passive telemetry stream; operators manually calculate and manage energy_per_ton offline without real-time feedback.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Hybrid energy management architecture—vehicle-level energy metering and local optimization (onboard), centralized KPI aggregation and operator feedback (tele-operator and safety services layers), with real-time synchronization via Telemetry and Tasking interfaces.

potential_sources: Vehicle energy metering must synchronize with Teleoperation KPI display (Telemetry interface dependency).
Tele-operator control recommendations must not conflict with vehicle-level safety constraints (SafetyAlert interface dependency).
Energy optimization logic must not degrade safety criticality (high safety criticality constraint).
coupling_check.notes: DP-C exhibits loose coupling: vehicle and tele-operator subsystems are decoupled via well-defined interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert). Energy optimization is localized to vehicle and tele-operator domains; no circular dependencies detected. Coupling is manageable via interface contracts.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Vehicle onboard systems have sufficient computational capacity to perform real-time energy metering and local optimization without impacting primary control loops.
ASSUMPTION: Network link (Telemetry, Tasking) provides sufficient bandwidth and latency (<500ms) for real-time KPI synchronization.
RISK: If vehicle energy sensors are inaccurate or drift over time, computed energy_per_ton will be unreliable, undermining acceptance criteria.
RISK: If tele-operator feedback loop is delayed or lossy, operators may not receive timely efficiency recommendations, reducing effectiveness.
RISK: Energy optimization logic may inadvertently conflict with safety-critical maneuvers (e.g., emergency braking); SafetyAlert interface must enforce safety precedence.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify in DP-C that energy optimization is subordinate to safety constraints: SafetyAlert interface shall have priority over Tasking recommendations. Define sensor accuracy requirements (±5% energy measurement) and network latency SLAs (≤500ms for Telemetry updates) to mitigate risks.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Top-level system integration boundary encompassing Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems. Responsible for end-to-end energy efficiency KPI achievement, including measurement, computation, feedback, and compliance reporting.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle → NGHE Solution: Energy consumption (kWh), work output (tonnage), operational state. Frequency: real-time (≥1 Hz). Used for KPI computation and operator feedback.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: NGHE Solution → Vehicle: Energy optimization recommendations (e.g., speed adjustment, load balancing). Frequency: event-driven or periodic (≤5 sec). Operator/tele-operator initiated or automated.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional: Vehicle → NGHE Solution (safety constraints, emergency signals); NGHE Solution → Vehicle (safety-gated control recommendations). Ensures energy optimization does not violate safety criticality.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Underlying communication substrate for Telemetry, Tasking, and SafetyAlert. Latency, bandwidth, and reliability requirements must support real-time KPI feedback and safety-critical signaling.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural top-level container for CR-NGHE-003. Energy efficiency is a system-level KPI requiring integration across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. Minimal diffusion: energy measurement logic is localized to Vehicle; KPI computation and feedback are localized to Teleoperation and Safety Services. No duplication or scattered ownership.

Vehicle can own energy metering and local optimization (sub-FR 003.1, 003.3 partial), but cannot own KPI computation (003.2) or compliance reporting (003.4) without diffusion. Assigning CR-NGHE-003 to Vehicle alone would scatter responsibility and reduce system-level visibility.

Teleoperation can own KPI display and operator feedback (sub-FR 003.2 partial, 003.3 partial), but cannot own vehicle-level energy metering (003.1) or compliance reporting (003.4) without diffusion. Assigning CR-NGHE-003 to Teleoperation alone would create a disconnected feedback loop.

Safety Services can own safety-gated energy optimization (sub-FR 003.3 partial), but cannot own measurement (003.1), KPI computation (003.2), or reporting (003.4). Assigning CR-NGHE-003 to Safety Services alone would fragment the energy efficiency capability.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: CR-NGHE-003 is a system-level KPI requirement that spans multiple subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services). No single subsystem can own the full requirement without significant diffusion. NGHE Solution, as the top-level integration boundary, is the appropriate owner. Sub-FRs (003.1–003.4) are allocated to specific subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) with clear ownership and interface contracts.
containment_risks: RISK: If NGHE Solution is treated as a passive container without explicit ownership of KPI computation and reporting, responsibility may diffuse to Vehicle or Teleoperation, leading to inconsistent metrics.
RISK: If sub-FR allocation is not clearly documented, Vehicle and Teleoperation teams may implement energy monitoring independently, creating redundancy and inconsistency.
RISK: If SafetyAlert interface is not rigorously enforced, energy optimization logic may inadvertently override safety constraints, violating high safety criticality.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Establish explicit ownership: NGHE Solution owns CR-NGHE-003 and sub-FRs 003.2 (KPI computation) and 003.4 (compliance reporting). Vehicle owns sub-FR 003.1 (energy measurement). Teleoperation owns sub-FR 003.2 (KPI display). Safety Services owns sub-FR 003.3 (safety-gated optimization). Define interface contracts (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert) with clear data schemas, latency SLAs, and safety precedence rules.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (hybrid energy management) directly addresses CR-NGHE-003 Refined. DP-C specifies how to achieve energy_per_ton ≤ E_target through vehicle-level metering, centralized KPI computation, and real-time operator feedback. The DP is logically sound, avoids circular dependencies, and is feasible within stated constraints (network latency, computational capacity). Coupling is manageable via interface contracts.

diffusion_risks: DIFFUSION RISK: If energy measurement logic is duplicated across Vehicle and Teleoperation, metrics may diverge. Mitigation: Vehicle owns measurement (sub-FR 003.1); Teleoperation consumes via Telemetry interface.

DIFFUSION RISK: If KPI computation is scattered across multiple modules, compliance reporting (sub-FR 003.4) may be inconsistent. Mitigation: NGHE Solution owns centralized KPI computation and reporting.

DIFFUSION RISK: If energy optimization recommendations are generated by both Vehicle and Teleoperation, conflicting commands may be issued. Mitigation: Tasking interface enforces single-source-of-truth for control recommendations; SafetyAlert ensures safety precedence.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate IM boundary for CR-NGHE-003. Sub-FRs are allocated to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services with clear ownership and interface contracts. Containment score for NGHE Solution is 95/100; diffusion is minimal (5/100). No scattered ownership or duplication detected.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: CR-NGHE-003 Refined is verifiable: energy_per_ton can be measured and compared against E_target over an agreed evaluation period. Verification requires: (1) accurate energy sensors on Vehicle (±5% accuracy), (2) reliable work output measurement (tonnage), (3) real-time KPI computation and logging, (4) compliance reporting to Site Manager. All are technically feasible within NGHE Solution scope.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Sub-FRs (003.1–003.4) are clearly allocated to specific modules: Vehicle (measurement), Teleoperation (display), Safety Services (safety-gated optimization), NGHE Solution (computation and reporting). Allocation is non-overlapping and covers the full requirement. Interface contracts (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert) define data flow and ownership boundaries.

recommended_revisions: Revise DP-C to explicitly state that SafetyAlert interface has priority over Tasking recommendations. Energy optimization shall not override safety-critical constraints.
Define sensor accuracy requirements (±5% energy measurement) and network latency SLAs (≤500ms for Telemetry updates) in DP-C assumptions.
Establish a detailed sub-FR allocation matrix (003.1–003.4) with ownership, interface contracts, and verification criteria for each sub-FR.
Add a traceability requirement: every energy optimization recommendation (Tasking) must be logged and auditable for compliance verification.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-003 Refined is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs (003.1–003.4) with distinct ownership and verification criteria. DP-C (hybrid energy management) is sound and feasible, but requires detailed design at the next level to specify: (1) vehicle-level energy metering architecture (sub-FR 003.1), (2) KPI computation algorithms and real-time display (sub-FR 003.2), (3) energy optimization logic and safety-gating (sub-FR 003.3), (4) compliance reporting and audit trails (sub-FR 003.4). Proceeding to the next decomposition level is necessary to allocate sub-FRs to specific modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) and define detailed DPs and IMs.
decision.justification_detail: The refined FR is clear and verifiable, but the DP is at a system-level abstraction. To ensure proper architectural packaging and avoid diffusion, each sub-FR must be decomposed further. For example, sub-FR 003.1 (energy measurement) requires detailed DP specifying sensor types, data acquisition rates, and calibration procedures. Sub-FR 003.2 (KPI computation) requires DP specifying algorithms, update frequency, and display format. Proceeding to the next level will enable detailed design and implementation planning.
next_level_sub_frs: CR-NGHE-003.1: The Vehicle shall continuously measure and log energy consumption (kWh) and work output (tonnage moved) via onboard sensors and data acquisition systems.
CR-NGHE-003.2: The Teleoperation system shall compute and display real-time energy_per_ton KPI to operators and tele-operators with update frequency ≥1 Hz.
CR-NGHE-003.3: The Safety Services system shall provide energy optimization recommendations to operators/tele-operators, subject to safety-critical constraints enforced via SafetyAlert interface.
CR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE Solution shall log and report energy efficiency metrics to Site Manager for compliance verification, with audit trail and traceability.

assumptions: Vehicle onboard systems have sufficient computational capacity to perform real-time energy metering without impacting primary control loops.
Network link (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert) provides sufficient bandwidth and latency (<500ms) for real-time KPI synchronization.
Energy sensors on Vehicle are accurate to ±5% and are calibrated regularly.
Site Manager and operators have access to real-time KPI display and compliance reporting tools.
Energy optimization logic is subordinate to safety constraints; SafetyAlert interface has priority over Tasking recommendations.
Customer-defined E_target is provided and agreed upon before evaluation period begins.
open_risks: RISK: Vehicle energy sensors may drift over time, causing energy_per_ton measurements to become unreliable. Mitigation: Define sensor calibration procedures and periodic validation.

RISK: Network latency or packet loss may delay KPI updates, reducing operator responsiveness. Mitigation: Define network SLAs and implement fallback mechanisms (e.g., local KPI computation on tele-operator workstation).
RISK: Energy optimization logic may inadvertently conflict with safety-critical maneuvers. Mitigation: Rigorous testing of SafetyAlert interface and safety-gating logic.
RISK: Operators may not understand or trust energy efficiency recommendations, leading to non-adoption. Mitigation: User interface design and operator training.

RISK: Energy efficiency may vary significantly across different worksite conditions (terrain, load type, operator skill), making E_target difficult to achieve consistently. Mitigation: Define evaluation period and conditions carefully; consider adaptive E_target based on worksite context.
evidence_needed: Detailed energy measurement architecture (sensor types, data acquisition rates, calibration procedures) for Vehicle sub-FR 003.1.
KPI computation algorithms and real-time display specifications for Teleoperation sub-FR 003.2.
Energy optimization logic and safety-gating rules for Safety Services sub-FR 003.3.
Compliance reporting and audit trail specifications for NGHE Solution sub-FR 003.4.
Network latency and bandwidth measurements to validate Telemetry, Tasking, and SafetyAlert interface SLAs.
Sensor accuracy validation and calibration procedures.
Operator acceptance testing and user interface feedback.
Field trial data demonstrating energy_per_ton ≤ E_target over agreed evaluation period.
